skill.name: tsdown
skill.description: Use tsdown to configure, migrate, and troubleshoot TypeScript and JavaScript library builds with Rolldown.
skill.summary: Configure fast TypeScript library builds with tsdown and Rolldown.
skill.author: antfu

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a very high score because it treated Markdown command examples, package links, and build configuration snippets as executable behavior. Review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, hidden network calls, or credential exfiltration, but the skill does include shell, publish, env-file, and filesystem examples that warrant a user warning.

medium_findings.title: Documented Shell Execution Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill contains Markdown examples that import child_process, call execSync for clean, test, and publish steps, and spawn tsdown in watch mode with shell enabled. This is legitimate bundler guidance, but agents should ask before running publish, destructive clean, or shell-backed commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in documentation examples, but they are not hidden executable skill code. The risk depends on an agent or user choosing to run the examples.

medium_findings.title: Environment File and Variable Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents env-file and process.env usage for tsdown builds. This is normal build configuration guidance, but it can expose secrets if users load broad .env files into bundles without filtering.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly reference environment variables and env files. No evidence shows credential exfiltration or automatic secret collection.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Most external command, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network hits are Markdown code fences, package format names, documentation links, or configuration snippets. I found no prompt injection, hidden network calls, or malicious runtime logic in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed these flagged lines are documentation examples or URLs, not executable behavior. The skill is Markdown-based and no injection phrases were found by targeted search.

low_findings.title: File Operation Examples in Build Hooks
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for rmSync, dynamic fs/promises imports, and readFileSync in tsdown configurations. These are expected for bundler recipes, but users should constrain paths and avoid applying examples to untrusted input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit but appear inside safe instructional examples. No evidence shows arbitrary path input or automatic execution by the skill.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Without Runtime Requests
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to license attribution, project links, npm package references, and documentation links. I found no evidence of runtime network access, telemetry, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation references and package links. No fetch, curl, webhook, or external reporting behavior was found in the reviewed evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Shell Execution Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains Markdown examples that import child_process, call execSync for clean, test, and publish steps, and spawn tsdown in watch mode with shell enabled. This is legitimate bundler guidance, but agents should ask before running publish, destructive clean, or shell-backed commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in documentation examples, but they are not hidden executable skill code. The risk depends on an agent or user choosing to run the examples.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment File and Variable Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents env-file and process.env usage for tsdown builds. This is normal build configuration guidance, but it can expose secrets if users load broad .env files into bundles without filtering.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly reference environment variables and env files. No evidence shows credential exfiltration or automatic secret collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: File Operation Examples in Build Hooks
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples for rmSync, dynamic fs/promises imports, and readFileSync in tsdown configurations. These are expected for bundler recipes, but users should constrain paths and avoid applying examples to untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit but appear inside safe instructional examples. No evidence shows arbitrary path input or automatic execution by the skill.

content.value_statement: Modern TypeScript libraries need fast builds, clean package exports, and reliable type declarations. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical tsdown guidance for configs, migrations, and troubleshooting.
actual_capabilities: Create tsdown configurations for ESM, CJS, IIFE, and UMD library output.
Explain entry points, output directories, source maps, minification, and clean options.
Guide migration from tsup to tsdown with option mapping and compatibility notes.
Configure declaration file generation, declaration maps, and Vue type support.
Help set up React, Vue, and WebAssembly library build recipes.
Document CI-aware options, hooks, plugins, and programmatic build usage.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a bundled tsdown executable.
It cannot verify every project dependency or package export without reading the repository.
Some examples include shell commands that should be reviewed before execution.
Experimental tsdown features may change as Rolldown and Oxc evolve.

use_cases.title: Set Up a New Library Build
use_cases.description: Create a tsdown config that emits ESM, CJS, type declarations, and package exports for npm publishing.

use_cases.title: Migrate from tsup
use_cases.description: Map existing tsup options to tsdown and identify behavior changes before replacing the build command.

use_cases.title: Tune Framework Packages
use_cases.description: Configure React, Vue, or WASM package builds with correct externals, declaration output, and source maps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Config
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a small package
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a tsdown config for my TypeScript library. It should build ESM and CJS output, generate declaration files, and clean dist first.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Build Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my tsdown configuration and explain what each option does. Point out settings that affect package exports, source maps, and type declarations.

prompt_templates.title: Migrate a Project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me migrate this package from tsup to tsdown. Compare the current build options, propose a tsdown config, and list behavior changes to test.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Release Build
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a tsdown release build for a monorepo package. Include CI-aware validation, external dependency handling, declaration maps, and safe prepublish checks.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks for a tsdown setup for a dual-format TypeScript package.
output_examples.output: A concise build plan with ESM and CJS outputs, declaration generation, clean output handling, and package export guidance.

output_examples.input: A team asks why source maps and declaration maps are behaving differently in CI.
output_examples.output: An explanation of tsdown source map options, declaration map behavior, and CI-aware settings to verify.

output_examples.input: A project owner asks whether a tsup config can be replaced directly.
output_examples.output: A migration checklist covering option mapping, dependency externalization, output validation, and tests to run before publishing.

best_practices: Review generated build commands before running clean, publish, or shell-backed workflows.
Keep package exports, declaration files, and emitted formats aligned with package.json.
Use CI-aware validation for package linting, type checks, and release builds.
anti_patterns: Do not load broad .env files into browser bundles without a strict prefix filter.
Do not copy release-script examples into projects without checking paths and publish targets.
Do not assume tsup behavior is identical without testing output files and package exports.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps AI assistants configure and explain tsdown builds for TypeScript and JavaScript libraries.

faq.question: Does it install tsdown for me?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The assistant may suggest install commands for you to approve.

faq.question: Can it migrate from tsup?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes migration guidance, option mapping, and compatibility notes for moving from tsup to tsdown.

faq.question: Does it support declaration files?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers declaration generation, declaration maps, compiler options, and Vue type generation.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The references include shell, publish, env-file, and filesystem examples. Review those commands before execution.

faq.question: Which assistants can use it?
faq.answer: The report marks it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
